programs.name: ADvantage Waiver Program
programs.tagline: Get paid home care services for your aging parent so they can avoid nursing home placement.

localEntities.name: Local county OKDHS offices for applications and assessments; case managers arrange services via regional providers

programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or managing medications, the ADvantage Waiver can pay for home care services to keep them out of a nursing home. The program covers personal care, homemaker services, and therapies — all provided in your parent's own home.

Your parent qualifies if they're 65+ (or younger with a physical disability), have monthly income under $2,199, and need Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment that they require daily assistance with basic activities). Because this is a Medicaid waiver program with limited slots, eligible applicants may face a waitlist even if they meet all requirements.

The specific services your parent receives depend on their individual care plan developed with a case manager. This could include help with bathing, meal preparation, light housework, or physical therapy — whatever keeps them safely at home instead of in institutional care.

summary: Need daily help with basic activities
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need Nursing Facility Level of Care — daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, mobility, toileting, or meal preparation due to physical or cognitive limitations
otherRequirements: Oklahoma resident and U.S. citizen or legal resident
Live in home or community setting

applicationGuide.summary: Call your local county OKDHS office to request an assessment — processing time varies due to waitlist.

steps.title: Contact your county OKDHS office
steps.description: Call to arrange a financial and medical assessment for your parent

steps.title: Complete financial eligibility interview
steps.description: Adult and Family Services specialist conducts phone interview about income and assets

steps.title: Get medical assessment
steps.description: OKDHS nurse conducts comprehensive assessment (in-home or by phone) using UCAT III tool to determine if your parent needs Nursing Facility Level of Care

steps.title: Wait for slot availability
steps.description: If eligible but no slots available, your parent's name goes on the statewide waitlist

steps.title: Develop care plan
steps.description: Once approved and slot opens, case manager creates individualized treatment plan and arranges services

applicationGuide.waitlist: Yes, statewide waitlist if all waiver slots filled — not guaranteed even if eligible
applicationGuide.tip: Gather medical documentation showing your parent's daily care needs before the assessment to speed the process

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts Toward $2,000 Limit
included: Cash value of life insurance policies
Real estate other than primary home
excluded: Home if spouse, minor child, or disabled child lives there

tiers.name: Seniors 65+
tiers.description: Frail elderly needing nursing facility level of care
tiers.coverage: Personal care, homemaker services, therapies, case management

tiers.name: Adults 19-64 with Physical Disability
tiers.coverage: Same services as seniors, can continue after age 65

tiers.name: Adults 19-64 with Developmental Disability
tiers.description: Developmental disability without intellectual disability or cognitive impairment

categories.name: Identity
items: Proof of citizenship or legal residency

categories.name: Financial
items: Property tax statements for home equity value
Life insurance policies with cash value

categories.name: Medical
items: Documentation of help needed with bathing, dressing, mobility
Physical therapy or occupational therapy records

contentSections.text: Limited waiver slots mean a waitlist exists statewide. Even if your parent qualifies, they may wait months or years for services to begin.
Dementia alone doesn't qualify — your parent must also need physical help with daily activities like bathing or dressing to meet Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for ADvantage?
faqs.answer: Yes, if her home equity is under $730,000 in 2025. The home is exempt while she lives there or intends to return. However, Oklahoma may recover costs from the home's value after she passes away through Medicaid Estate Recovery.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over $2,199 per month?
faqs.answer: He won't qualify for ADvantage. The income limit is strict at $2,199 monthly countable income. Consider spending down assets or consulting an elder law attorney about income planning strategies.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist for services?
faqs.answer: Oklahoma doesn't publish waitlist times. It depends on when current participants leave the program and new slots become available. Contact your county OKDHS office for current waitlist estimates in your area.

faqs.question: What's the difference between needing help and qualifying medically?
faqs.answer: Your parent must need Nursing Facility Level of Care — meaning they require daily assistance that would otherwise require nursing home placement. A nurse assesses this using specific criteria about activities like bathing, dressing, and mobility.

faqs.question: Can we choose Mom's home care workers?
faqs.answer: The case manager arranges services with approved providers in your area. You may have some choice among available providers, but options depend on what's available in your county and your parent's specific care plan.

faqs.question: What happens if Mom's condition improves?
faqs.answer: Annual redeterminations check both financial and medical eligibility. If she no longer needs Nursing Facility Level of Care, she'll lose ADvantage services. However, if her condition worsens, services can be adjusted.

programs.name: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.tagline: All medical care, meals, and transportation in one program if your parent needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay home.

localEntities.name: Valir PACE: Oklahoma City Adult Day Center, Shawnee Adult Day Center, Seminole County.
LIFE PACE: Defined service area (details on lifepace.org).

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay home, PACE provides everything through one team: all medical care, prescription drugs, hot meals, transportation, and personal care services. Because PACE covers all services under one program, your parent won't need to coordinate between separate doctors, specialists, or agencies — one interdisciplinary team manages everything.

PACE is only available in specific areas of Oklahoma: Oklahoma City, Shawnee, and parts of Seminole County through Valir PACE, plus LIFE PACE's service area. Your parent must qualify for nursing home-level care (needing daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management) and meet SoonerCare income and asset limits. If your parent qualifies for Medicaid, PACE has no copays or deductibles. Private pay costs approximately $4,000–$5,000/month.

The trade-off: your parent must use PACE as their sole healthcare provider. They cannot see outside doctors or specialists except in emergencies, and using unauthorized services means paying out-of-pocket.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need nursing facility level of care (assessed through Uniform Comprehensive Assessment Tool - daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or similar care needs)
otherRequirements: Live in PACE provider service area
PACE team determines you can be safely served at home
Agree to use PACE as sole healthcare provider
Not enrolled in Medicare Advantage or hospice

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local PACE provider directly for assessment — processing time varies by provider and requires clinical evaluation.

steps.title: Check if you live in a PACE service area
steps.description: Valir PACE serves Oklahoma City, Shawnee, and specific ZIP codes in Seminole County (74830, 74837, 74849, 74867, 74868, 74884). LIFE PACE serves a different defined area.

steps.title: Contact the PACE provider directly
steps.description: Call Valir PACE through valir.com/pace or LIFE PACE through lifepace.org to start the assessment process.

steps.title: Complete clinical assessment
steps.description: PACE team conducts medical and functional assessment using the Uniform Comprehensive Assessment Tool (UCAT) Part III to determine nursing facility level of care.

steps.title: Financial eligibility review
steps.description: Provide income and asset documentation to verify SoonerCare eligibility limits.

steps.title: Interdisciplinary team review
steps.description: PACE team determines if your parent can be safely served in the community and approves enrollment.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified — varies by provider and requires clinical assessment
applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible — varies by provider and service area demand
applicationGuide.tip: Start by confirming your ZIP code is in the service area before beginning the assessment process.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Real estate other than primary home
excluded: Burial plots and prepaid funeral expenses

offices.name: LIFE PACE
offices.address: Check lifepace.org for service area details

categories.name: Financial
items: Property tax records for real estate

contentSections.text: PACE is only available in limited areas of Oklahoma. Check your ZIP code first — many counties have no PACE providers.
Your parent cannot use outside doctors while in PACE except for emergencies. Any unauthorized medical services will be your parent's financial responsibility.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep seeing her current doctor if she joins PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE requires your mom to use their interdisciplinary team as her sole healthcare provider. She cannot see outside doctors, specialists, or other providers except in true emergencies. Using unauthorized services means paying out-of-pocket.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is just over the SoonerCare limit?
faqs.answer: She can still enroll in PACE as a private pay participant for approximately $4,000–$5,000 per month. This covers all the same services but without Medicaid assistance for costs.

faqs.question: Does PACE cover nursing home care if Mom needs it later?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE covers nursing home-level services both at home and in facilities if your mom's needs change. The same PACE team coordinates all care regardless of setting.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house while in PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home is exempt from asset limits for PACE eligibility. She can keep her house regardless of its value.

faqs.question: What happens if we move to a different city in Oklahoma?
faqs.answer: PACE is only available in specific service areas. If you move outside your PACE provider's service area, your mom would need to disenroll and find other healthcare coverage.

faqs.question: How is PACE different from regular Medicare and Medicaid?
faqs.answer: PACE combines all Medicare and Medicaid benefits under one provider team, plus adds services like transportation, meals, and adult day care. Instead of coordinating between multiple providers, one PACE team manages everything.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Program
programs.tagline: If your parent's Medicare costs are straining their budget, this program can eliminate premiums and cut drug costs to $12.65 per prescription.

localEntities.name: Local OKDHS Human Services Centers statewide.

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to afford Medicare premiums, deductibles, or prescription drugs, Oklahoma's Medicare Savings Program can eliminate these costs entirely. The program has four tiers — the lowest tier (QMB) covers all Medicare premiums, deductibles, and coinsurance, while higher tiers cover at least Medicare Part B premiums. All tiers also provide Extra Help for prescription drugs, capping costs at $12.65 per medication in 2026.

Typical savings run around $185 monthly, though this varies by tier and your parent's Medicare usage. Because Oklahoma has strict asset limits (around $2,000 for individuals, $3,000 for couples in lower tiers), this program works best for parents with limited savings but whose income falls just above Medicaid limits.
programs.savingsSource: Based on ~$185 monthly savings example from program data

summary: Income varies by tier (example: $1,215/month for lowest tier)
otherRequirements: Must be eligible for Medicare Part A (even if not enrolled)
Part B required for SLMB and QI tiers
structuredEligibility.povertyLevelReference: Up to 135% FPL for highest tier

applicationGuide.summary: Apply at your local OKDHS Human Services Center or call the Senior Health Insurance Counseling line — processing time not specified but QI tier is first-come, first-served.

steps.title: Gather Medicare and financial documents
steps.description: You'll need proof of Medicare Parts A and B, income statements, and bank statements. Important: Add back any Medicare Part B premium deducted from Social Security — this doesn't count toward income limits.

steps.title: Contact your local OKDHS center or call the state helpline
steps.description: Visit your nearest OKDHS Human Services Center in person, or call Oklahoma Insurance Department Senior Health Insurance Counseling at 800-763-2828.

steps.title: Let the state determine your tier
steps.description: Oklahoma will place your parent in the highest tier they qualify for — you don't need to choose. They may qualify for a higher tier than expected.

applicationGuide.waitlist: QI tier may have waitlist due to federal funding caps — apply even if unsure about qualification
applicationGuide.tip: Income limits update every April, so check current limits in OKDHS Appendix C-1 before applying.

tiers.name: QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: Medicare Part A premiums (if applicable), Part B premiums and deductible, all coinsurance and deductibles, plus Extra Help for drugs

tiers.name: SLMB (Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.incomeLimit: Higher than QMB (exact limits in OKDHS Appendix C-1)
tiers.coverage: Medicare Part B premiums plus Extra Help for drugs

tiers.name: QI (Qualifying Individual)
tiers.coverage: Medicare Part B premiums plus Extra Help (drugs capped at $12.65 each in 2026)

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Real estate other than primary home

categories.name: Medicare Proof
items: Medicare card showing Parts A and B

categories.name: Financial
items: Social Security statements (add back Part B premium)
Pay stubs if working (over half of employment income excluded)
Documentation of all assets and investments

contentSections.text: QI tier requires annual reapplication and is first-come, first-served with funding caps. If your parent qualified before, they get priority, but apply early when renewal opens.

When calculating income, remember to add back the Medicare Part B premium deducted from Social Security — this doesn't count toward the income limit. Also, if your parent works, over half of employment income is excluded.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for this program?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your parent's primary home doesn't count toward asset limits regardless of value. They can also keep one car, household furnishings, and pre-paid funeral trusts up to $10,000.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: Income limits update every April, and there are four different tiers with different limits. Oklahoma will automatically place him in the highest tier he qualifies for. Also remember that if he works, over half of employment income doesn't count toward limits.

faqs.question: Does this replace Medicare or give Mom full Medicaid?
faqs.answer: No, this works alongside Medicare. Your parent keeps their regular Medicare coverage, but the state pays their premiums, deductibles, and coinsurance (depending on tier). It's not full Medicaid with long-term care coverage.

faqs.question: How much will this actually save on prescriptions?
faqs.answer: All tiers include Extra Help for prescription drugs. In 2026, this caps costs at $12.65 per medication. The exact savings depend on what medications your parent currently takes and their current Medicare drug plan.

faqs.question: What happens if Mom gets denied but her situation changes?
faqs.answer: She can reapply anytime her income or assets change. Income limits also update every April, so someone who didn't qualify one year might qualify the next. The QI tier has annual reapplication anyway.

faqs.question: If Dad is married but Mom doesn't need this program, how do asset limits work?
faqs.answer: Oklahoma has spousal protection rules. The spouse who doesn't need the program can keep up to $148,620 in assets. This prevents couples from having to impoverish both spouses for one to qualify.
